title: The World Between Worlds — Corbin's Mundus Imaginalis and the Guide of Light
author: Henry Corbin

body.text: Henry Corbin spent a lifetime translating the lost organs of Western perception. Not inventing them — finding them already articulated in the texts of Suhrawardi, Ibn Arabi, Mulla Sadra Shirazi, and the Sufi masters of Iran. What he recovered was not a doctrine but an organ: the active Imagination, a cognitive faculty with its own noetic dignity, and the world it perceives, the mundus imaginalis. This is not fantasy, not myth in the debased sense, not 'imaginary' as we use the word to mean unreal. It is an intermediate ontological region, as real as matter and as real as spirit, and the soul must be trained to see it.

Between the world of pure spiritual Lights (Luces victoriales, the world of the 'Mothers' in the terminology of Ishraq) and the sensory universe, at the boundary of the ninth Sphere (the Sphere of Spheres) there opens a mundus imaginalis which is a concrete spiritual world of archetype-Figures, apparitional Forms, Angels of species and of individuals; by philosophical dialectics its necessity is deduced and its plane situated; vision of it in actuality is vouchsafed to the visionary apperception of the active Imagination.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, pp. 42-43

body.text: Notice the precision: this world is deduced by dialectics, not by wishful thinking. Its necessity is philosophical before it is visionary. The mundus imaginalis is not a luxury for mystics — it is a metaphysical requirement for any cosmology that recognizes both matter and spirit. Without an intermediate term, the two cannot communicate. The active Imagination is the bridge-faculty for a bridge-world. Corbin's deepest claim is that Western philosophy collapsed this intermediate region, leaving only the dualism of res extensa and res cogitans, and that this collapse is the unacknowledged source of the modern crisis of meaning.

This suprasensory Orient governs the primary phenomenon of the Gnostic's orientation toward his country of origin. The Orient-origin identified with the center, with the heavenly north pole, heralds access to the beyond, where vision becomes real history, the history of the soul, and where every visionary event symbolizes a spiritual state; or, as the Ishraqiyun say, it is the climate 'where what is bodily becomes spirit and what is spiritual acquires a body.'
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 44

body.text: Here Corbin introduces the eighth climate — the region 'where what is bodily becomes spirit and what is spiritual acquires a body.' This is the precise ontological signature of the imaginal: it is neither the abstraction of pure spirit nor the density of mere matter. It is a realm where form and meaning interpenetrate so fully that every event is both a fact and a symbol. To enter it is to become oriented toward the origin — the 'suprasensory Orient' — and that orientation changes the very texture of what one perceives.

The 'eighth' keshvar, the mystical Earth of Hurqalya with emerald cities; it is situated on the summit of the cosmic mountain, which the traditions handed down in Islam call the mountain of Qaf. This is the paradisal Earth of Light, the world of Hurqalya, an Orient intermediate between the 'lesser Orient,' which is the soul's rising to the highest point of its desire and consciousness, and the 'greater Orient,' which is the further spiritual Orient, the pleroma of pure Intelligences.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, pp. 43-44

body.text: The geography is symbolic, but the symbolism is not arbitrary. The eighth climate is 'beyond the mountain of Qaf' — beyond the spheres that constitute the sensory cosmos. To find it, one does not travel in space. One travels in state. And the guide who shows the way is not a human teacher but a celestial interlocutor: the Perfect Nature, the Daena, the Guide of Light. This figure is the soul's own heavenly counterpart, the form that the soul will wear when it has become what it essentially is.

It is the heavenly entity, the philosopher's Angel, conjoined with his star, which rules him and opens the doors of wisdom for him, teaches him what is difficult, reveals to him what is right, in sleeping as in waking.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 17

body.text: This is the classical Hermetic definition of Perfect Nature, drawn from the Ghayat al-Hakim. The Angel is not an external being in the sense of a separate entity; it is the soul's own celestial root, the 'star' under which the philosopher was born. But neither is it merely a part of the psyche. It has its own reality, its own initiative, its own pedagogy. It teaches, opens doors, reveals. The relation between the seeker and the Guide is dialogical — a true I-Thou relation, not a psychological introjection.

When I wished to bring to light the science of the mystery and modality of Creation, I came upon a subterranean vault filled with darkness and winds... Lo and behold, a person then appeared to me in my sleep in a form of the greatest beauty. He said to me: 'Take a lamp and place it under a glass to shield it from the winds; then it will give thee light in spite of them...' At that I said, 'Who then art thou?' He answered: 'I am thy Perfect Nature. If thou wishest to see me, call me by my name.'
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 18

body.text: Hermes' own vision establishes the pattern: the seeker is in darkness, in a vault, in the winds of confusion. The Guide appears in a dream — the intermediate state between waking and sleeping, the natural threshold of the imaginal. And the instruction is precise: contain the light, protect it from the dispersing winds of multiplicity. The lamp under glass is the heart shielded from distraction. The name of the Guide is the secret that, once known, makes invocation possible. 'Call me by my name' is an invitation to relationship, to the unus-ambo.

Having cut in two the thread spun by a spider, the prince puts it together again, saying: 1 x 1. This is also the formula that we suggested above, because he who deciphers it holds the key to the secret that preserves him both from pseudomystical monism (whose formula is 1 = 1) and from abstract monotheism which is content to superimpose an Ens supremum on the multitude of beings (n + 1).
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 25

body.text: The unus-ambo formula — one times one — is Corbin's most elegant philosophical cipher. It avoids two errors. The first is monism (1 = 1), which dissolves the lover into the Beloved and loses the dialogical tension that makes spiritual life real. The second is mere theism (n + 1), which sets God above the world as a separate supreme being and never heals the rupture. The unus-ambo says: the seeker and the Guide are two, but their relation is not addition; it is multiplication. Each is fully itself, and their union generates something that neither could be alone.

My image looks at me with my own look; I look at it with its own look. Their relation is this unus-ambo in which each of the two simultaneously assumes the position of the I and the self — image and mirror.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 17

body.text: The mirror-gaze reciprocity is the lived experience of the unus-ambo. Neither term is passive. The image is not a reflection I possess — it has its own gaze, its own look, and it looks at me with the same look I use to look at it. This is the structure of theophanic perception: the visionary does not project an image and then worship it. The image meets the visionary halfway, in the imaginal space between. The relation is mutual and creative.

When the circle of the face has become pure, it effuses lights as a spring pours forth its water... Before you, before your face, there is another Face also of light, irradiating lights... In reality this Face is your own face and this sun is the sun of the Spirit (shams al-ruh) that goes to and fro in your body. Next, the whole of your person is immersed in purity, and suddenly you are gazing at a person of light (shakhs nurani) who is also irradiating lights... This person of light before you is called in Sufi terminology the suprasensory Guide (moqaddam al-ghayb).
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 85

body.text: Najm Kobra's description is not metaphor. It is phenomenology — a precise account of what happens when the active Imagination opens onto the imaginal world. The face becomes a circle of purity, light effuses from it, and a second Face appears before it — the Face of the Guide. The grammar is crucial: 'this Face is your own face' — identity — 'and this sun is the sun of the Spirit' — transcendence. The Guide is both the seeker's deepest Self and something more than the seeker. The unus-ambo is not a speculative formula; it is a visionary fact.

I fell passionately in love with a young girl... the flame of love within me became extraordinarily intense. My breath exhaled flames of fire. And each time I breathed out fire, lo and behold, from the height of heaven someone was also breathing out fire which came to meet my own breath... at last I understood that it was my witness in Heaven.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, pp. 86-87

body.text: This is the most moving passage in the entire tradition. The young Najm Kobra falls in love — human, passionate, consuming love. The flame of desire rises from his chest. And from heaven, another flame descends to meet it. The meeting of the two fires is the moment of recognition: the earthly beloved was the occasion, but the real Beloved is the Witness in Heaven. The shahid (witness) is the form that the soul's own depth takes when it becomes visible. Love is the fire that makes the form appear.

Then who art thou, whose beauty outshines all other beauty ever contemplated in the terrestrial world? I am thine own Daena. I was loved, thou hast made me more loved still. I was beautiful, thou hast made me still more beautiful.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 30

body.text: The Daena — the Zoroastrian prototype of the Guide — is the soul's own self, encountered at the Chinvat Bridge. But notice the reciprocity again: 'I was loved, thou hast made me more loved still.' The seeker's love adds to the beauty of the Beloved. This is not a static hierarchy where the divine gives and the human receives. The relationship is co-creative. The Guide becomes more beautiful through being loved. The soul's devotion is not a one-way street; it contributes to the very radiance of the heavenly form.

Thou, my lord and prince, my most holy angel, my precious spiritual being, Thou art the Spirit who gave birth to me, and Thou art the Child to whom my spirit gives birth... Thou who art clothed in the most brilliant of divine Lights... may Thou manifest Thyself to me in the most beautiful of epiphanies, show me the light of Thy dazzling face, be for me the mediator... lift the veils of darkness from my heart...
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 21

body.text: Sohravardi's psalm to Perfect Nature captures the paradox at the heart of the unus-ambo: 'Thou art the Spirit who gave birth to me, and Thou art the Child to whom my spirit gives birth.' The Guide is both origin and offspring, both parent and child. This is not a logical contradiction resolvable by dialectic; it is the structure of the imaginal relationship itself, where linear causality gives way to mutual generation. The soul gives birth to its Angel at the same moment that the Angel gives birth to the soul.

How does the seeker prepare for this encounter? Through the dhikr — the practice of remembrance that Corbin describes as the furnace in which the dark envelope of the lower self is consumed. The dhikr is not mere repetition; it is the fire that separates light from darkness within the soul, that liberates the captive particle of divine light from its entanglement with the nafs ammara, the commanding self.

Of all spiritual practices... the dhikr is the practice most apt to free spiritual energy, that is, to allow the particle of divine light which is in the mystic to rejoin its like... The fire of the dhikr is visualized as a pure and ardent blaze, animated by a rapid upward movement... It sets fire to all that is there to be consumed, and sheds light on any darkness it may encounter. If light is there already, the two lights associate with each other and there is light upon light.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, pp. 73-74

body.text: The dhikr operates at the level of subtle physiology. It is a fire that burns the dross — the dark envelope — and frees the light. But it does not create the light; it reveals what was already there. 'If light is there already, the two lights associate with each other and there is light upon light.' This is the same structure we saw in Najm Kobra's account: the human fire meets the heavenly fire. The dhikr is the practical discipline that makes the unus-ambo a lived reality rather than a speculative idea.

What we are speaking of is a counterpart, a celestial, transcendent counterpart, rather than a 'double'; the idea of this heavenly partner is antithetic to the Double... psychological analysis shows this Double to be the manifestation of the personal unconscious, hence belonging to the functions of the lower psyche, that is, the nafs ammara, the dark envelope, the shadow, exactly what the dhikr has to destroy by fire so that the Guide of light may become visible.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 95

body.text: Corbin makes a critical distinction here — one that saves the entire project from psychological reductionism. The Guide is not the Double. The Double is the shadow, the dark envelope, the personal unconscious — precisely what must be burned away. The Guide, by contrast, is the celestial counterpart, the transcendent root of the soul, which was never part of the personal psyche. To mistake the Guide for a projection of the unconscious is to collapse the imaginal into the merely psychological — the very error Corbin spent his career opposing.

Green is the color that outlasts the others. From this color emanate flashing, sparkling rays... the heart is a subtle organ which reflects suprasensory things and realities that revolve around it... the heart is a light in the depths of the well of nature, like Joseph's light in the well into which he was thrown.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 78

body.text: The visio smaragdina — the emerald vision — is the chromatic signature of the awakened heart. Green is the colour that persists through all the transformations of vision. It is the colour of the Prophet's mantle, of the Green Island of the Hidden Imam, of Khezr the mysterious guide who appears at the threshold of the eighth climate. The heart is Joseph's light in the well: light thrown down into the depths of nature, waiting to be drawn up. The emergence of the green light is the sign that the well is being illuminated from within.

The suprasensory Witness, the suprasensory Guide, the suprasensory Scales: this is what you are shown when you close your eyelids. According to whether what appears to you is light or darkness, your witness (shahid) is light or darkness.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, pp. 89-90

body.text: The Witness is also the Scales. The figure that appears in the visionary state is not just a guide — it is a measure. The state of the soul is reflected in the form that appears. Light for light, darkness for darkness. This is why the dhikr must first burn away the dark envelope: the Guide that appears to a soul still entangled in the nafs ammara will not be the Guide of Light but the Doppelganger, the shadow wearing the mask of the Angel. Purification is not moralism; it is discernment, the precondition for authentic vision.

There is a difference in that the visionary apperception presupposes the opening of the inner eye by the removal of the veil which darkened it, whereas the feeling of mystic experience (dhawq) is caused by a transmutation of the being and of the spirit... The five senses are changed into other senses.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 80

body.text: The transformation is total. The five senses themselves become organs of suprasensory perception. Not through addition — through transmutation. The same ears that hear traffic learn to hear the music of the spheres; the same eyes that scan a page learn to see the light of the Guide. The active Imagination is not a sixth sense competing with the five; it is the five transfigured, operating at a different ontological level — the level of the mundus imaginalis.

The growth of the subtle organism, the physiology of the man of light, progresses through the seven latifa, each of which is one of the seven prophets of your being; the cycle of birth and initiatic growth is homologous to the cycle of prophecy.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, p. 125

body.text: Ala al-Dawla Simnani's doctrine of the seven subtle organs (latifa) is the culmination of the man of light's physiology. Each subtle organ corresponds to a prophet of Islam, and each prophet represents a stage of the soul's transformation. The body of flesh is not abolished; it is grown through, into a body of light. As the seven prophets appear in sacred history, the seven latifa appear in the microcosmic history of the individual soul. Spiritual growth is not an escape from embodiment but the transubstantiation of embodiment into subtle form.

The black light is the light of the pure Essence in its ipseity, in its abscondity; the ability to perceive it depends on a spiritual state described as 'reabsorption in God' (fana fi'llah)... The perfection of contingent being is to regress to its basic negativity, and to come to know through its own unknowingness. It means to know with the certainty of experience that the summum of knowledge is unknowingness, for here there is infinite disproportion.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, pp. 111-117

body.text: The journey does not end in vision. At the horizon of the imaginal world lies the black light — the darkness of the pure Essence, the divine Ipseity that cannot be seen because it is the source of all seeing. The black light is not the absence of light; it is light so intense that it appears as darkness to the created eye. The visio smaragdina gives way to the via negativa. The Guide of Light leads the soul to the threshold where even the Guide is absorbed, and the soul knows by unknowing. This is the apophatic completion of the imaginal path: the eighth climate opens onto the ninth, which has no climate at all.
The suprasensory Witness, the suprasensory Guide, the suprasensory Scales: this is what you are shown when you close your eyelids.
body.reference: The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism, pp. 89-90

body.text: Corbin's recovery of the mundus imaginalis is not a historical curiosity. It is a diagnosis and a prescription. The modern West collapsed the intermediate world and lost the faculty that perceives it, leaving only the dualism of matter and spirit, fact and fantasy, science and superstition. The consequence is a civilization that has images everywhere — in advertising, cinema, screens — and imaginal perception nowhere. We are flooded with the imaginary because we have lost the imaginal. Corbin's work is an invitation to rebuild the organ, to reopen the eighth climate, to meet again the Guide who has been waiting for us beyond the mountain of Qaf, at the place where the fire of our longing meets the fire that descends from heaven.
